# Transcrição: 04-Delivery

- **Fonte:** 04-Delivery
- **Duração:** 08:28
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 02:59]

Okay, last thing before we get to the strategies. I have to admit something to you. Delivery matters. The delivery of humor is pretty important. In fact, here's a crazy but true thing. Sometimes, actually, many times, something can be made funny just by the way you say it. One person says it one way, another person says it another way. An audience laughs in one case and does not laugh in another case. I was once teaching a workshop and a guy named Chris really wanted to be funny. He really wanted to make everyone in the workshop laugh, so he crafted a story, came to the workshop, and told the story. We did not laugh. Chris was sort of devastated, and I pointed out to him that he had crafted the humor well. He just hadn't delivered it properly, and so I actually took his paper, read his story aloud, and made everyone laugh, showing him that delivery matters a lot. It turns out that did not help Chris. That was not a good way to teach someone how to be funny. It just made him feel bad. It was true that the way you say something really can make something funny or not funny. There are a multitude of ways that people can make something funny by the way they say it, including volume, vocalization, pitch, pacing, pausing, all of those things. But what I like to think of is four strategies, four things you're going to need to think about when you're delivering humor to an audience. The first is confidence, and it's the hardest one, because it really can't be taught. But when you take the stage and you think something's funny, if you think you have crafted something that is humorous, you have to believe it in your heart when you say it. If you don't believe it, people know you don't believe it, and even if it is funny, they will not laugh. If you don't believe it's funny, they will not believe it's funny. So you have to make sure that when you're saying something out loud and you expect a laugh, you need to be saying it as if you expect the laugh, confidence. It's the hardest one. I have yet to find a way to teach confidence. I can tell you this though, it can be faked. You can fake confidence until you feel it. You can pretend that you are confident in the material that you're delivering, pretend until you feel it, go on stage imagining what it is like to be a confident person, embodying all the qualities of a confident person, even if inside you are a quivering mass of jelly.

## [03:01 - 06:02]

If on the outside you appear confident and the audience believes you are confident, they are more likely to laugh. Strategy number two, armor. You have to be prepared for people to not laugh. You have to have a thick skin. You have to be willing to say something funny, whether it's on stage or at dinner with your friends or walking down the fairway on the golf course. You say something that you think is funny and they don't laugh. You have to have armor and thick skin to try again with something else. One of my favorite comedy podcasts is called Good One. Jesse Fox of Vulture produces this and he always asks the comics at the end a question that I love. Tell us a joke that you know is funny, that no one else has ever found funny. What he's really saying is, what is the joke that you have offered to the world that they have not laughed but because you have armor, you have continued to try to land that joke. That's what you have to do. If you think it's funny and no one's laughing, you have to have that armor, that thick skin, that allows you to try again. You can't allow silence to silence you. You can't allow an audience who does not respond to you to cause you to stop speaking. Armor is really important, not everything you say that you think is funny will produce a laugh. You have to accept that and move on. The third strategy I offer you is disdain. You should despise an audience that does not laugh when you know you're being funny. Years ago, I was competing in a moth story slam at the Brooklyn Historical Society. My first visit to this venue, I had a funny, somewhat raunchy story to tell the audience and when I arrived there, I discovered that the audience at the Brooklyn Historical Society was mostly blue-haired old ladies. They did not think my story was funny at all. They were the wrong audience for that story. I hated them for it. I hated their inability to find humor and something that had produced laughs in audiences before and I knew was objectively funny. But rather than allowing their silence to impact me, my confidence, my ability to move forward, I just chose to hate them. I just chose to hate them for their inability to find humor in the world, their lack of senses of humor. Sometimes you're going to find yourself in front of an audience that should be laughing because you are saying funny things and they will not. Maybe it's the setting, maybe it's the time of day, maybe they're just terrible people. There's nothing wrong with disdain. Hate them, leave and move on. The last strategy I'm going to offer is the most important

## [06:02 - 08:28]

one. Strategy number four is your ears. Ultimately, your audience really will tell you if you're funny. There are going to be rare instances when you find yourself in front of blue-haired ladies where you get to hate them and move on and ignore their responses. But in most cases, if an audience doesn't laugh, what you said wasn't quite funny enough or perhaps it wasn't funny at all. But what you need to do is start listening carefully. Listen to when people laugh at the things you say and then ask yourself, what did I do there? Did I say it differently? Did I reorder the words in such a way that it produced the laugh? Pay attention to how other people get audiences to laugh. What is that person doing to make people laugh that I might also do? How are they alternating the way they speak? How are they pausing? How are they assembling words? How are they setting up jokes? How are they calling back jokes? Did they just raise their voice a little bit and that made people laugh? Watch Seinfeld. Jerry Seinfeld, about 90% of the time, is simply raising the octave of his voice to produce a laugh. You could do the same. Now, maybe you don't want to do that. Maybe you don't want to sound like Jerry Seinfeld, but maybe it wouldn't be so bad to sound a little bit like Jerry Seinfeld. Listen with your ears carefully to how you make people laugh and how other people make people laugh. The more you listen to why people laugh and the more times you ask yourself the question, what caused people to laugh at that particular moment, the funnier you'll become, the more strategies you will have, the more you will be able to move your voice and move your body and move the words around to produce humor. Your ears are your most powerful tool when it comes to humor. Learn from other people and learn from the audiences, the audiences that are laughing at you and the audiences that are laughing at other people. Become an outstanding listener and analyze what you hear. That is going to help you become a funnier person. Now, we can learn some strategies.
